preview_text:
The model projects a dominant Canada victory, assigning the side an 86% win probability on a neutral ground. Qatar faces only a 14% chance of drawing and effectively no modeled path to victory, a stark asymmetry that reflects the input data's implied gap in underlying strength.

The W/D/L spread reveals a fairly lopsided contest in expected terms. Canada's win probability towers over the draw likelihood, suggesting the model envisions a match unlikely to reach parity. A draw remains plausible at 14%, but Qatar's near-zero win probability indicates the forecast sees little genuine competitive balance; the outcome space is largely Canada win or stalemate.

The model and market diverge meaningfully. The model backs Canada at 86%, while sportsbooks settle at 75% and Polymarket at 75%—roughly an 11 percentage point gap favoring the model's confidence. Conversely, the market prices Qatar's chances at 9% to 8.5%, compared to the model's 0%. This dashboard makes no claim as to which source is correct; the gap reflects either model overconfidence in Canada or market inflation of Qatar's tail-case probability.
